named_technique: Joint prime-square obstruction mod 44100 for consecutive powerful triples (Beckon 2019 + p=5, p=7)
bridge_lemma: Atomic obstruction: for prime p, if x mod p^2 ∈ {p, 2p, …, (p-1)p} then ¬ Powerful x because p∣x but p^2∤x (already proven in slot 1325 for p=2 and p=3, extended here to p=5 yielding 4 lemmas and p=7 yielding 6 lemmas). Sequential omega case-splits then give n%4=3 ∧ n%9∈{0,7,8} ∧ n%25∈T25 ∧ n%49∈T49 where T25 and T49 are the 13- and 31-element triple-residue sets respectively. Joint CRT over {4,9,25,49} yields the 1209/44100 admissible residue classes mod 210².
informal_proof_outline:
Goal: under Powerful n ∧ Powerful (n+1) ∧ Powerful (n+2), prove the conjunction (n%4=3) ∧ (n%9 ∈ {0,7,8}) ∧ (n%25 ∈ T25) ∧ (n%49 ∈ T49).

The mod 4 and mod 9 components are already proved in slot 1325 (theorem erdos_364_mod36) — the proof structure decomposes into 3 obstruction lemmas (not_powerful_of_mod4_eq2, not_powerful_of_mod9_eq3, not_powerful_of_mod9_eq6) plus omega case-splits on n%4 and n%9.

For the new mod 25 component, prove 4 atomic obstruction lemmas:
  - not_powerful_of_mod25_eq5: x%25=5 → ¬Powerful x (since 5∣x but 25∤x)
  - not_powerful_of_mod25_eq10: x%25=10 → ¬Powerful x
  - not_powerful_of_mod25_eq15: x%25=15 → ¬Powerful x
  - not_powerful_of_mod25_eq20: x%25=20 → ¬Powerful x
Proof of each: from hpow 5 Nat.prime_five (by omega: 5∣x), conclude 25∣x, contradict x%25=r∈{5,10,15,20}.

For mod 49, prove 6 atomic obstruction lemmas:
  - not_powerful_of_mod49_eq{7,14,21,28,35,42}: x%49=r → ¬Powerful x (since 7∣x but 49∤x)

Assembly via case-split + omega: by_contra; case-split on n%25 (25 cases via omega — for each non-T25 residue, identify which of n, n+1, n+2 has the forbidden mod-25 residue and apply the corresponding lemma); same for mod 49 (49 cases). Total: ~74 cases, all dispatched by omega + the obstruction lemmas.

Mathlib infrastructure verified resident: Nat.prime_two, Nat.prime_three, Nat.prime_five, Nat.prime_seven (all in Mathlib.Data.Nat.Prime); omega; native_decide. The proof closely mirrors slot 1325's structure — same template, more primes.
candidate_lemmas: L_mod25_eq_5: ∀ (x : ℕ), x % 25 = 5 → ¬ Powerful x. Proof: have h5 : 5 ∣ x := by omega; have : 25 ∣ x := by have := hpow 5 Nat.prime_five h5; norm_num at this; exact this; omega.
L_mod25_eq_10: ∀ (x : ℕ), x % 25 = 10 → ¬ Powerful x. Same template.
L_mod25_eq_15: ∀ (x : ℕ), x % 25 = 15 → ¬ Powerful x. Same template.
L_mod25_eq_20: ∀ (x : ℕ), x % 25 = 20 → ¬ Powerful x. Same template.
L_mod49_eq_7: ∀ (x : ℕ), x % 49 = 7 → ¬ Powerful x. Proof: have h7 : 7 ∣ x := by omega; have : 49 ∣ x := by have := hpow 7 Nat.prime_seven h7; norm_num at this; exact this; omega.
L_mod49_eq_14, L_mod49_eq_21, L_mod49_eq_28, L_mod49_eq_35, L_mod49_eq_42: same template with r ∈ {14, 21, 28, 35, 42}.

L_decompose_mod25: ∀ n, Powerful n → Powerful (n+1) → Powerful (n+2) → n%25 ∈ T25 := {0,1,2,6,7,11,12,16,17,21,22,23,24}. Proof: by_contra; case-split on n%25 ∈ Fin 25; in each non-T25 case (n%25 ∈ {3,4,5,8,9,10,13,14,15,18,19,20,23,24}∩complement-T25), identify which of n, n+1, n+2 has residue in {5,10,15,20} mod 25 and apply the corresponding L_mod25_eq_r.

L_main_mod44100: ∀ n, Powerful n → Powerful (n+1) → Powerful (n+2) → (n%4=3) ∧ (n%9 ∈ {0,7,8}) ∧ (n%25 ∈ T25) ∧ (n%49 ∈ T49). Trivial conjunction of L_decompose_mod4, L_decompose_mod9, L_decompose_mod25, L_decompose_mod49.
L_main_mod44100_set: ∀ n, the 3 conditions above imply n%44100 ∈ S where |S|=1209. Proof: native_decide enumerates Fin 44100 to verify.
honest_disclaimer:
MEGA-3 systematic extension of Beckon 2019 (mod 36, slot 1325) to all prime-square obstructions p^2 ≤ 100. Prime powers p^k≤100 with k≥2 are: 4,8,9,16,25,27,32,49,64,81. Empirical computation shows that going beyond p^2=4 for p=2 (i.e. to 8, 16, 32, 64) adds NO information about triples — the constraint n%4=3 already forces all 3 of n, n+1, n+2 mod 4 ∈ {3,0,1}, none of which is the excluded mod-4 residue 2; higher powers of 2 give no additional constraint because the v_2=1 obstruction only kicks in at residues ≡ 2 mod 4. Same for p=3: mod 9 captures everything; mod 27 and mod 81 lift trivially. So the four 'active' moduli are exactly p^2 for p ∈ {2,3,5,7} (the primes with p^2 ≤ 100), giving joint modulus 4·9·25·49 = 210² = 44100. Computational verification: triple-residue mod 4 = {3} (1 element); mod 9 = {0,7,8} (3); mod 25 = {0,1,2,6,7,11,12,16,17,21,22,23,24} (13); mod 49 = 31 elements (above). Joint mod 44100 = 1209 elements, smallest 71. Beckon mod 36 gives 3 residues = 3·1225 = 3675 of these residues mod 44100, so we eliminate 3675-1209 = 2466 = 67.1% of Beckon-admissible residue classes. Beckon's smallest triple residue n=7 (also 27, 35) is killed by mod 49 (7 mod 49 = 7 ∈ excluded; 28 mod 49 = 28 ∈ excluded; 35 mod 49 = 35 ∈ excluded). The smallest surviving residue is n=71 (10× larger than Beckon's 7). Bayesian probability estimates: P(target_resolved=1) ≈ 0.70 — the proof is structurally identical to slot 1325 (proven), just with more atomic obstruction lemmas and a larger omega case-split. P(compiled_partial) ≈ 0.18 — some atomic lemmas compile, others left sorry. P(compile_failed) ≈ 0.07. P(compiled_no_advance) ≈ 0.05. Risk: native_decide on Fin 44100 may time out; mitigation is to state the WEAKER conjunctive form (mod 4, 9, 25, 49 separately) rather than the explicit 1209-element Finset enumeration. Per CLAUDE.md rule 10: HAVE FAITH IN ARISTOTLE.

Cross-critique log (7 rounds):
  R1 (Codex enumeration): Enumerate prime powers p^k ≤ 100 with k ≥ 2: {4, 8, 9, 16, 25, 27, 32, 49, 64, 81}. For each, compute powerful residues (= those with v_p(r) ≠ 1). Confirmed.
  R2 (Codex triple residues): For each modulus M ∈ {4,9,25,49}, compute T_M = {n : n,n+1,n+2 all powerful mod M}. T_4={3}, T_9={0,7,8}, T_25 (13 elements), T_49 (31 elements). Going beyond p^2 (e.g. mod 8, 27, 81): projects exactly to T_{p^2}, so no additional info. CONFIRMED active moduli = exactly {4, 9, 25, 49}.
  R3 (Grok-4 cross-check): Verified the analysis via independent calculation. Joint admissible set is 1209 residues mod 44100, smallest = 71. Beckon's mod 36 result is recovered as a coarser projection.
  R4 (Codex absolute lower bound): Smallest admissible residue jumps from 7 (Beckon) to 71. Within any interval [N, N+44100], only 1209 starting positions allowed (vs Beckon's 3675). For very small n (say n < 1000), the candidates are {71, 99, 107, 151, 171, 207, 323, 331, 351, 367, 387, 423, 431, 547, 575, 611, 647, 675, 711, 799, 827, 871, 891, 899, 907, 927}.
  R5 (Grok adversarial novelty check): Searched Beckon 2019 (Rose-Hulman), Chan 2025 (arXiv:2503.21485), She 2025 (arXiv:2507.16828), van Doorn 2026 (arXiv:2605.06697), Erdős 1975, Mollin-Walsh 1986, Granville 1986. NONE perform the joint mod 25 + mod 49 analysis. Chan and She use mod 9 only (for the cube-centered subcase). Beckon stopped at mod 36. The arXiv search 'consecutive powerful numbers mod 25 mod 49' returns NO papers using this joint modulus.
  R6 (Codex Lean formalization): The proof structure replicates slot 1325 exactly. 10 new atomic lemmas (4 for mod 25, 6 for mod 49) each follow the same 3-line proof template. Omega + native_decide handle the case-split assembly. Estimated Lean proof length: 80-120 lines.
  R7 (Grok final polish): Confirm the statement form. The weaker conjunctive form (n%4=3 ∧ ... ∧ n%49 ∈ T49) is preferable to the explicit Finset {71,99,107,...} form because (a) it directly mirrors the proof structure, (b) it avoids requiring native_decide on Fin 44100 which may time out, (c) it's mathematically equivalent. Recommend the conjunctive form for the .txt statement.

Literature anchors:
  - Beckon, E. 'On consecutive triples of powerful numbers', Rose-Hulman Undergraduate Math Journal 20 no. 2 (2019), Article 3 — establishes n mod 36 ∈ {7,27,35} ONLY.
  - Chan, T.H. 'A note on three consecutive powerful numbers', Integers 25 (2025) Paper A7, arXiv:2503.21485 — uses mod 9 for cube-centered subcase only.
  - She, T. 'Nonexistence of consecutive powerful triplets around cubes with prime-square factors', arXiv:2507.16828 (2025) — uses mod 9 and LTE only; no mod 25 or mod 49.
  - Granville, A. 'Powerful numbers and Fermat's last theorem', C.R. Math. Acad. Sci. Soc. R. Can. 8 (1986), 215-218 — ABC-conditional finiteness; no explicit modular obstruction.
  - Mollin, R.A. and Walsh, P.G. 'A note on powerful numbers, quadratic fields and the Pellian', C.R. Math. Acad. Sci. Soc. R. Can. 8 (1986), 109-114 — original conjecture, no explicit modular obstructions beyond cubes.
  - Erdős, P. 'Problems and results on number theoretic properties of consecutive integers and related questions', Proc. Fifth Manitoba Conf. on Numerical Math (Winnipeg 1975), 25-44.

Domain relations: subsumes=['Beckon (2019) mod 36 closure, slot 1325']; subsumed_by=['full Erdős 364: ¬ ∃ n, Powerful n ∧ Powerful (n+1) ∧ Powerful (n+2) (OPEN)']; related_open=['E938 (3-term AP of consecutive powerful)', 'general d=1 case is Erdős 364']; sharpens=['Beckon (2019)']; structural-analogue=['Beckon for d=1 powerful triples extended to all primes p with p^2 ≤ 100']

Paired LLM: grok-4-1-fast + codex-5.3 (MEGA-3 deep iteration, 7 rounds)
